options.content: The biceps would contract, shortening to flex the forearm upward, while the triceps would relax.
The biceps would remain relaxed, but the triceps would contract further to extend the forearm upward.
The biceps would contract further, flexing the forearm upward, while the triceps would contract slightly to control the movement.
Both the biceps and triceps would contract simultaneously, generating equal opposing forces.

questions.specification:
The table shows events occurring during the contraction of a skeletal muscle fibre.

| | Process | Immediate cause |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **I** | Power stroke | ATP binding to the myosin head |
| **II** | Myosin detachment | Tropomyosin moving to cover the actin filament |
| **III** | Exposure of binding sites | Calcium ions binding to troponin |
| **IV** | Myosin head re-cocking | Release of ADP and inorganic phosphate |

Which row correctly matches a process with its immediate cause?

labeledSubSubTopics.title: B3.3.2 Sliding filament model of muscle contraction
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks to identify the correct step in the sliding filament model of muscle contraction, specifically focusing on the roles of calcium ions, troponin, and the ATP/ADP cycle in the cross-bridge formation and power stroke.

questions.specification:
The electron micrograph shows a sarcomere in the myofibril of skeletal muscle. The brackets X and Y indicate two different regions of the sarcomere.

![sarcomere](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-biology-new-2ae8563e-d99e-4e8e-b7fa-585bac751c6d-1771815806714/c3b502e7-ce61-4037-b4c5-93daa0639f2a.jpeg)

How would the lengths of regions X and Y change when the myofibril contracts?

| | X | Y |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| A | narrower | no change |
| B | no change | narrower |
| C | narrower | narrower |
| D | no change | no change |

labeledSubSubTopics.path: B - Form and Function > B3.3 Muscle and motility (HL) > B3.3.2 Sliding filament model of muscle contraction
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B3.3.2 Sliding filament model of muscle contraction
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question specifically tests the sliding filament model of muscle contraction by asking how specific regions of the sarcomere (H-zone and A-band) change in length during contraction.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A - Unity and Diversity > A2.2 Cell structure > A2.2.11  Drawing and annotation based on electron micrographs
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A2.2.11  Drawing and annotation based on electron micrographs
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question requires the interpretation and analysis of an electron micrograph of a striated muscle sarcomere.

options.markscheme: Incorrect. This would be correct only if Y were the A-band (constant length) and X a region that shortens; here Y is taken to represent the H-zone, which shortens during contraction.
Incorrect. If Y is the H-zone it becomes narrower, but X (the A-band) does not change length.

Correct. X represents the H-zone, which becomes narrower as thin filaments slide inwards. Y represents the I-band, which also becomes narrower as the region containing only thin filaments decreases during contraction.
Incorrect. During contraction both the I-band and H-zone shorten; only the A-band remains the same length.

questions.specification:
An electron microscope was used to measure the structural components of a sarcomere within a myofibril under two different conditions. The results are recorded in the table below.

| Measurement | State 1 ($\mu m$) | State 2 ($\mu m$) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Total sarcomere length | 3.2 | 2.6 |
| Thick (myosin) filament length | 1.6 | 1.6 |
| H-zone width | 0.8 | 0.2 |
| Distance between Z-line and thick filaments | 0.8 | 0.5 |

Which conclusion is most accurate regarding the transition from State 1 to State 2?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: B - Form and Function > B3.3 Muscle and motility (HL) > B3.3.2 Sliding filament model of muscle contraction
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B3.3.2 Sliding filament model of muscle contraction
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question provides quantitative data on sarcomere measurements (length, H-zone, thick filaments) during a transition between states. This data is the classic evidence used to support and explain the sliding filament model of muscle contraction, where the sarcomere and bands shorten while the filaments themselves maintain a constant length.

options.content: The muscle fibre is relaxing as evidenced by the reduction in H-zone width.
The muscle fibre is contracting as evidenced by the reduction in the length of the thin filaments.
The muscle fibre is contracting as evidenced by the reduced Z-line to A-band edge distance.
The muscle fibre is relaxing as evidenced by the constant length of the myosin filaments.

| B | Execution of jump (extension) | Contracted | Relaxed |
| C | Execution of jump (extension) | Relaxed | Contracted |
| D | Preparation for jump (flexion) | Contracted | Contracted |

|---------------------|----------------------|
| Before ATP Addition | 2.5                  |
| After ATP Addition  | 2.1                  |

labeledSubSubTopics.path: B - Form and Function > B3.3 Muscle and motility (HL) > B3.3.2 Sliding filament model of muscle contraction
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B3.3.2 Sliding filament model of muscle contraction
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question focuses on sarcomere shortening after ATP addition and asks what this indicates about contraction, directly aligning with the sliding filament model (actin–myosin interactions causing sarcomere length decrease).

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C - Interaction and Interdependence > C1.2 Cell respiration > C1.2.1 ATP as the molecule that distributes energy within cells
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C1.2.1 ATP as the molecule that distributes energy within cells
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (b) asks for ATP’s molecular role in contraction; while primarily muscle physiology, it also relates to ATP as an energy-distribution molecule within cells (ATP binding/hydrolysis driving cross-bridge cycling).

parts.content: Describe the change in sarcomere length after ATP addition, and state what this indicates about contraction (sliding filament mechanism).
parts.markscheme:
- Sarcomere length decreases from $2.5\ \mu\text{m}$ to $2.1\ \mu\text{m}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Indicates the fibre has contracted / sarcomere shortening has occurred <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Acceptable indication linked to sliding filament: increased overlap of actin and myosin / Z-lines move closer together / reduced I-band (and/or H-zone) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain the role of ATP in muscle contraction at the molecular level.
parts.markscheme:
- ATP binds to myosin heads causing them to detach from actin (breaks actin–myosin cross-bridges) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- ATP is hydrolysed to ADP + Pi, providing energy to cock/reset (energize) the myosin head for the next interaction with actin <marks>1 mark</marks>
- ADP and Pi release is associated with the power stroke as myosin pulls actin (acceptable extension) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- ATP is required to prevent/terminate rigor (cross-bridges remain attached without ATP) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Based on the diagram of the cross-section of a sarcomere, describe the structure and function of the actin and myosin filaments in muscle contraction.
parts.markscheme:
- Actin filaments are thin protein filaments <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Composed primarily of actin protein <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Contain binding sites for myosin heads <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Anchored to Z-lines at ends of sarcomere <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Myosin filaments are thick protein filaments <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Composed of myosin protein <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Have protruding heads that can bind to actin <marks>1 mark</marks>

- During contraction, myosin heads attach to actin and pull filaments toward sarcomere center, shortening the muscle <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award marks for correct structural features and functional aspects separately</note>

<examTip>Use specific terminology like 'Z-lines' and 'sarcomere' rather than general descriptions</examTip>

questions.specification: Which anatomical principle must be applied so that muscles can effectively exert a pulling force on bones to create movement?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: B - Form and Function > B3.3 Muscle and motility (HL) > B3.3.5 Roles of skeletons as anchorage for muscles and as levers
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B3.3.5 Roles of skeletons as anchorage for muscles and as levers
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks about the principle that allows muscles to exert force on bones. Topic B3.3.5 specifically addresses skeletons as anchorage for muscles and as levers, which is the physical basis for how muscles pull on bones to create movement.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: B - Form and Function > B3.3 Muscle and motility (HL) > B3.3.3 Role of the protein titin and antagonistic muscles in muscle relaxation
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B3.3.3 Role of the protein titin and antagonistic muscles in muscle relaxation
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question highlights that muscles exert a 'pulling' force. A fundamental anatomical/physiological principle linked to this is that muscles can only contract (pull) and cannot push, necessitating antagonistic pairs for controlled movement (Topic B3.3.3).

options.content: Muscles must be physically anchored to bones with tendons
options.markscheme: Muscles attach to bones via tendons, allowing contraction to pull on bones and produce movement; ligaments connect bone to bone.

options.content: Muscles must be arranged in antagonistic pairs
options.markscheme: Antagonistic pairs allow movement in opposite directions at a joint, but do not describe the attachment principle that allows muscles to pull on bones.

options.content: Joints between bones must be synovial
options.markscheme: Synovial joints permit a wide range of movement, but muscles can still pull on bones via tendons at other joint types.

options.content: Muscles must be physically anchored to bones with ligaments
options.markscheme: Ligaments connect bone to bone, not muscle to bone; muscle-to-bone attachment is via tendons.

labeledSubSubTopics.title: B3.3.2 Sliding filament model of muscle contraction
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The data show A-band length stays constant while I-band and overall sarcomere length decrease, which is classic evidence for the sliding filament model of muscle contraction (thick filaments constant length, increased overlap).

options.content: Thin filaments shorten by about $50\%$ causing the reduced sarcomere length.
options.markscheme: Incorrect: thin (actin) filaments slide; filament length does not decrease.

options.content: Thick filaments keep the same length, supporting the sliding filament theory.
options.markscheme: Correct: the A-band (thick filament length) remains constant while the sarcomere shortens.

options.content: A-band stays constant because myosin stays centred at the M-line.
options.markscheme: Incorrect: being centred at the M-line is true, but the A-band is constant because thick filament length does not change; the data do not directly show M-line centring.

options.content: Muscle shortens by folding of protein heads within the I-band.
options.markscheme: Incorrect: the I-band decreases due to increased overlap of actin and myosin, not folding of myosin heads.

questions.specification: Which statement best describes a motor unit?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: B - Form and Function > B3.3 Muscle and motility (HL) > B3.3.4 Structure and function of motor units in skeletal muscle
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B3.3.4 Structure and function of motor units in skeletal muscle
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks for the function of a motor unit, which is explicitly covered under the structure and function of motor units in skeletal muscle.

options.content: A single motor neuron and all the muscle fibers it innervates
options.markscheme: Correct: this is the definition/description of a motor unit.

options.content: A synapse between a motor neuron and one muscle fiber
options.markscheme: Incorrect: this describes a neuromuscular junction, not the whole motor unit.

options.content: A protein that transports oxygen in muscle tissue
options.markscheme: Incorrect: oxygen transport is carried out by proteins such as myoglobin/hemoglobin, not motor units.

options.content: An organelle that stores ATP for muscle contraction
options.markscheme: Incorrect: ATP is not stored in a dedicated organelle; motor units are not involved in ATP storage.

parts.content: Describe the role of myosin and actin filaments in muscle contraction.
parts.markscheme:
- Myosin heads bind to actin filaments to form cross-bridges <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Myosin heads undergo a power stroke, pulling actin filaments towards the center of the sarcomere <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Sliding of actin filaments past relatively stationary myosin shortens the sarcomere (e.g. Z lines move closer; I band/H zone decrease) <marks>1 mark</marks>

- This sliding filament mechanism results in muscle contraction <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Award credit for reference to ATP hydrolysis providing energy for cross-bridge cycling / detachment <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Award credit for reference to Ca$^{2+}$ binding to troponin moving tropomyosin to expose actin binding sites <marks>1 mark</marks>

<examTip>Use specific biological terminology like 'cross-bridges' and 'power stroke' to demonstrate understanding</examTip>

questions.specification:
The table below shows average range of motion (ROM) for the human hip joint measured using a goniometer. | Movement | Average ROM
(degrees) | | :--- | :--- | | Flexion | 120 | | Extension | 20 | | Abduction | 45 | | Adduction | 30 | | External
Rotation | 60 |

labeledSubSubTopics.path: B - Form and Function > B3.3 Muscle and motility (HL) > B3.3.7 Range of motion of a joint
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B3.3.7 Range of motion of a joint
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question centers on comparing and explaining hip joint ranges of motion (ROM) and factors affecting ROM across populations, which directly matches joint range of motion.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: B - Form and Function > B3.3 Muscle and motility (HL) > B3.3.6 Movement at a synovial joint
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B3.3.6 Movement at a synovial joint
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Parts b and c ask about synovial joint structures (components reducing friction, joint capsule, ligaments) and how they enable movement and stability—core to movement at a synovial joint.

parts.content: Using the table, deduce which hip movement has the smallest range of motion and one anatomical reason why this movement is limited.
parts.markscheme:
Extension has the smallest range of motion at $20^\circ$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

Limited by tension in anterior ligaments (e.g. iliofemoral ligament) OR anterior joint capsule OR shape of acetabulum <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: List two components of the hip joint that function to reduce friction and protect joint surfaces.
parts.markscheme:
Synovial fluid lubricates the joint to reduce friction between articulating surfaces <marks>1 mark</marks>

Articular cartilage covers bone ends to reduce wear OR absorb shock <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how ligaments and the joint capsule contribute to the stability of the hip joint without preventing mobility.
parts.markscheme:
Ligaments restrict excessive movement to prevent dislocation but allow normal motion <marks>1 mark</marks>

Joint capsule encloses the joint and supports it while still permitting rotation and flexion <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: A researcher compares hip joint range of motion in two populations: sprinters and elderly adults. Predict two differences the researcher might observe and explain the biological reason for each.
parts.markscheme:
Sprinters may have greater ROM due to regular flexibility training OR elasticity of connective tissues <marks>1 mark</marks>

Elderly may have reduced ROM due to cartilage degeneration OR lower synovial fluid production OR stiffness in tendons/ligaments <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification: What changes occur to the Z-lines and the H-zone when a skeletal muscle fiber contracts?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: B - Form and Function > B3.3 Muscle and motility (HL) > B3.3.2 Sliding filament model of muscle contraction
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B3.3.2 Sliding filament model of muscle contraction
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks about changes to Z-lines and the H-zone during skeletal muscle contraction, which is explained by the sliding filament model (sarcomere shortening, H-zone narrowing, Z-lines moving closer).

options.content: The Z-lines move further apart and the H-zone becomes wider
The Z-lines move closer together and the H-zone becomes narrower
The Z-lines move closer together and the H-zone becomes wider
The Z-lines move further apart and the H-zone becomes narrower

questions.specification:
The table below lists various components of the human elbow joint and their primary functional roles.

| | Structure | Primary Functional Role |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| A | Joint capsule | Connects the biceps muscle to the radius |
| B | Cartilage | Reduces friction and absorbs shock between bones |
| C | Ligaments | Contract to pull on the ulna during flexion |
| D | Synovial fluid | Provides $$Ca^{2+}$$ ions to trigger muscle contraction |

Which row correctly matches the structure to its function?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: B - Form and Function > B3.3 Muscle and motility (HL) > B3.3.6 Movement at a synovial joint
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B3.3.6 Movement at a synovial joint
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks to identify the correct function for structures found in the human elbow joint, which is a primary example of a synovial joint. Topic B3.3.6 specifically covers movement and the roles of components (cartilage, synovial fluid, joint capsule, ligaments) within a synovial joint.

questions.specification: What is the role of calcium ions (Ca²⁺) in muscle contraction?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: B - Form and Function > B3.3 Muscle and motility (HL) > B3.3.2 Sliding filament model of muscle contraction
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B3.3.2 Sliding filament model of muscle contraction
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question describes the molecular mechanism of muscle contraction, specifically the role of calcium ions in the sliding filament model, where they bind to troponin to expose myosin-binding sites on actin.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C - Interaction and Interdependence > C2.1 Chemical signalling (HL) > C2.1.3 Hormones, neurotransmitters, cytokines and calcium ions
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C2.1.3 Hormones, neurotransmitters, cytokines and calcium ions
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: This topic explicitly lists calcium ions as signaling molecules; their role in triggering muscle contraction is a primary example of their function as second messengers/signaling ions.

options.content: They provide the energy for contraction by binding to ATP.
They trigger the release of ATP from the mitochondria.
They bind to troponin, causing a conformational change that exposes the binding sites on actin.
They interact with myosin to form cross-bridges with actin.

questions.specification:
Figure 2a: Electron micrographs and schematic diagram showing sarcomere structure in cardiac muscle.

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/pWe5L_pF7-2Y0HBWHuLwr.jpeg)

Figure 2b: Table summarizing the frequency of mutations in genes encoding cardiac contractile proteins and their associated cardiomyopathies.

| Gene  | Protein     | Percent of HCM cases | Percent of DCM cases | Percent of RCM cases | Associated Cardiac Diseases | 
|-------|-------------|----------------------|----------------------|----------------------|-----------------------------| 
| TNNT2 | Troponin T  | 5-15%                | 3-6%                 | 3-8%                 | HCM, DCM, RCM, LVNC         | 
| TNNI3 | Troponin I  | 5%                   | <1%                  | 3-17%                | HCM, DCM, RCM               | 
| TNNC1 | Troponin C  | 1%                   | 1%                   | Unknown              | HCM, DCM, RCM               | 
| TPM1  | Tropomyosin | 5%                   | <1-2%                | 3%                   | HCM, DCM, RCM, LVNC         | 
| ACTC1 | Actin       | <5%                  | <1%                  | 8%                   | HCM, DCM, RCM, LVNC, ASD    | 

- HCM: Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy
- DCM: Dilated Cardiomyopathy
- RCM: Restrictive Cardiomyopathy
- LVNC: Left Ventricular Non-compaction Cardiomyopathy
- ASD: Atrial Septal Defect

labeledSubSubTopics.path: B - Form and Function > B3.3 Muscle and motility (HL) > B3.3.2 Sliding filament model of muscle contraction
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B3.3.2 Sliding filament model of muscle contraction
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Parts b and d focus on sarcomere proteins (troponin/tropomyosin) and Ca2+-regulated actin–myosin interaction, which is core to the sliding filament model of muscle contraction.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: B - Form and Function > B2.3 Cell specialization > B2.3.9 Adaptations of cardiac muscle cells and striated muscle fibres (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B2.3.9 Adaptations of cardiac muscle cells and striated muscle fibres (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The figures and questions are explicitly about cardiac muscle ultrastructure (sarcomeres) and how changes to sarcomeric proteins relate to cardiac function/disease.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: D - Continuity and Change > D1.2 Protein synthesis > D1.2.11 Mutations that change protein structure
labeledSubSubTopics.title: D1.2.11 Mutations that change protein structure
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part f asks how mutations in genes encoding contractile proteins alter protein function/structure leading to cardiomyopathies, aligning with mutation-caused protein structural changes.

parts.content: Using Figure 2a (panel C), list two proteins involved in contraction regulation and state their respective functions.
parts.markscheme:
- Troponin ($TnI$/$TnT$/$TnC$): binds $Ca^{2+}$, causing tropomyosin to move/shift <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Tropomyosin ($Tpm$): blocks actin–myosin binding until a $Ca^{2+}$ signal is received <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for each correct protein and function pair, up to 2 marks.</note>

parts.content: Compare the prevalence of TNNT2 mutations in HCM and DCM using Figure 2b.
parts.markscheme:
- HCM prevalence is 5–15% and DCM prevalence is 3–6% <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Prevalence is higher in HCM than in DCM (HCM > DCM) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The ranges overlap (5–6%), but the maximum reported prevalence is higher in HCM (15% vs 6%) <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award marks only if comparison is quantified or directly states the relationship. Max 2.</note>

parts.content: Explain the role of $Ca^{2+}$ ions in the interaction between actin and myosin based on Figure 2a (panel C).
parts.markscheme:
- $Ca^{2+}$ binds to troponin (e.g., $TnC$), causing a conformational change in the complex <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Tropomyosin shifts/moves, exposing myosin-binding sites on actin <marks>1 mark</marks>
- This allows myosin heads to bind to actin (cross-bridge formation) <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Max 2.</note>

parts.content: Identify one gene from Figure 2b associated with all three conditions: HCM, DCM, and RCM.
parts.markscheme:
- TNNT2 
- _**OR**_ TNNI3 
- _**OR**_ TNNC1
- _**OR**_ TPM1 
- _**OR**_ ACTC1 <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Accept any one of the five genes listed as all are associated with all three conditions in the table.</note>

parts.content: Using Figures 2a and 2b, describe how mutations in sarcomeric proteins can lead to cardiomyopathies.
parts.markscheme:
- Structural: mutations can disrupt thin/thick filament assembly or force transmission, leading to reduced contractile force and chamber dilation (DCM) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Regulatory: mutations in troponin/tropomyosin can alter $Ca^{2+}$ sensitivity or switching of binding sites, causing hypercontractility and/or thickened ventricular walls (HCM) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Energetic/mechanical: mutations can alter cross-bridge cycling/ATP use, causing inefficient contraction and/or impaired relaxation/diastolic filling (RCM) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Mutations can cause myofibrillar disarray and subsequent fibrosis/remodelling, contributing to cardiomyopathy phenotypes <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Max 3.</note>

questions.specification:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/cff88407-71ff-49c9-97a2-1fd87aca6c2b)

Motor units play an important role in the control of skeletal muscle contraction strength and precision.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: B - Form and Function > B3.3 Muscle and motility (HL) > B3.3.4 Structure and function of motor units in skeletal muscle
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B3.3.4 Structure and function of motor units in skeletal muscle
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks specifically about how motor units regulate strength and precision of skeletal muscle contraction (recruitment/size principle, firing frequency, fine vs gross control), which is the focus of this leaf topic on motor units in skeletal muscle.

parts.content: Describe what is meant by a motor unit.
parts.markscheme: - A motor unit consists of a motor neuron and all the muscle fibers it innervates. <marks>4 marks</marks>

parts.content: Describe how recruitment of motor units affects the strength of a muscle contraction.
parts.markscheme:
- The strength/force of muscle contraction is controlled by the number of motor units activated (recruited). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- More motor units recruited results in stronger muscle contractions. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Increased firing rate/frequency of action potentials in motor neurons leads to stronger contractions (temporal summation). <marks>2 marks</marks>

parts.content: Explain how the size of motor units is related to the precision and force of muscle contractions, using named examples.
parts.markscheme:
- Small motor units have fewer muscle fibers per motor neuron. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Small motor units allow for more precise/fine control because activating one motor unit produces a smaller change in force. <marks>2 marks</marks>
- Example of precise control: muscles of the fingers or eyes. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Large motor units have more muscle fibers per motor neuron. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Large motor units are used for gross movements requiring more force because activating one motor unit produces a larger change in force. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Example of gross/forceful movement: leg (e.g. quadriceps) or back muscles. <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification: Which of the following correctly describes the role of actin in muscle contraction?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: B - Form and Function > B3.3 Muscle and motility (HL) > B3.3.2 Sliding filament model of muscle contraction
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B3.3.2 Sliding filament model of muscle contraction
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks specifically about the role of actin in muscle contraction, which is the core concept of the sliding filament model where actin thin filaments slide past myosin thick filaments.

options.content: Actin binds ATP to release energy for contraction.
Actin forms thin filaments that slide past myosin during contraction.
Actin contains the binding sites for calcium ions during muscle contraction.
Actin forms the thick filaments that pull on the thin filaments.

labeledSubSubTopics.title: B2.3.9 Adaptations of cardiac muscle cells and striated muscle fibres (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks to identify a muscle type based on its control mechanism (voluntary). Topic B2.3.9 covers the specialized features and adaptations of cardiac and striated (skeletal) muscle fibers, which includes their classification as voluntary or involuntary.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C - Interaction and Interdependence > C3.1 Integration of body systems > C3.1.7 Output from the cerebral hemispheres to muscles through motor neurons
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C3.1.7 Output from the cerebral hemispheres to muscles through motor neurons
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: This topic discusses how the cerebral hemispheres (responsible for conscious/voluntary thought) send output through motor neurons to skeletal muscles, explaining the biological basis of 'voluntary control'.
